coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive and rapid detection of Hg2+ in milk based on β-galactosidase driven colloidal motors
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kang, Sheng
Zheng, Yong
Zhu, Baohua
Wu, Yingjie
He, Qiang
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we present a new β-galactosidase-powered Janus colloidal motor for the active and rapid detection of Hg2+ in milk. The colloidal motor was fabricated by depositing a thin gold layer on one side of the self-assembled polyelectrolyte capsule, followed by immobilizing β-galactosidase and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots asymmetrically on the other side of the polyelectrolyte capsule. β-Galactosidase on the surface of the gold side can decompose the lactose in milk into glucose and galactose, leading to active motion. Furthermore, since nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots on the colloidal motor’s surface can be quenched by Hg2+ in milk, such colloidal motors are found to serve as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions. Benefiting from the efficient movement of the colloidal motors, the interaction between the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and the quenched substrate is greatly enhanced, thus improving the detection efficiency and accuracy of Hg2+. Under the optimized conditions, the colloidal motors achieved a linear detection range of 0.031–15 μM and the low detection limit of 9.1 nM. The colloidal motors are proving to be an attractive multifunctional sensor platform, providing a valuable means of monitoring Hg2+ contamination in dairy products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Janus colloidal motor
Autonomous movement
β-Galactosidase
Carbon quantum dots
Hg2+ detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Adsorption of N-CQDs by electrostatic interaction
Efficiency and selectivity of Hg2+ detection
Synthesis and characterization of β-gal-powered colloidal motors
Movement study of β-gal-powered colloidal motors
β-gal-powered colloidal motors-based fluorescent chemosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+
Building a smartphone sensing platform for rapid detection of Hg2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22172044 , U23A20342 ), Key R&D Program of Heilongjiang Province, China (Grant No. 2022ZX02C23 ), Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. YQ2022B005 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Schematic illustration of the hierarchical assembly of β-gal-powered colloidal motors. (B) TEM image of N-CQDs. The inset shows the size distribution of the N-CQDs. (C) Zeta potential of different samples. (D) SEM and corresponding EDS mapping images of β-gal-powered colloidal motors. (E) TEM image of β-gal-powered colloidal motors. The inset is a local magnification of β-gal-powered colloidal motors. (F) Size distribution of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors. (G) FTIR of β-gal-powered colloidal motors and Janus capsules. (H) Confocal laser scanning microscope image of β-gal-powered colloidal motors. (I) Fluorescence microscopic image of β-gal-powered colloidal motors (a) and β-gal-powered colloidal motors + Hg2+ (b). (J) UV–vis absorption spectra of β-gal-powered colloidal motors + Hg2+.

Autonomous motion behavior of β-gal-powered colloidal motors under different reaction conditions. (A) Schematic illustration of the motion of β-gal-powered colloidal motors. (B, C) Movement trajectories and MSD versus time interval of β-gal-powered colloidal motors under different fuel concentrations over 10 s. (D) Glucose concentration as a function of time during substrate breakdown. (E, F) The corresponding DL and velocity of β-gal-powered colloidal motors in the presence of different lactose concentrations. (G) pH stability of β-galactosidase immobilized micromotors and free enzyme. (H, I) MSD versus time interval and corresponding DL of β-gal-powered colloidal motors at different pH.

(A) UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra of β-gal-powered colloidal motors in aqueous solutions. (B) Fluorescence quenching efficiency (I 0 -I)/I 0 of β-gal-powered colloidal motors in the presence of different metal ions (C) Selective fluorescence response of aqueous β-gal-powered colloidal motors solution towards 30 μM Hg2+ (blue bars) and mixed solutions of 30 μM Hg2+ and other metal ions (red bars). (D) Comparison of fluorescence quenching efficiency between β-gal-powered colloidal motors and N-CQDs. (E) Fluorescence spectra of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors with different concentrations of Hg2+ under excitation at 360 nm. (F) The dependence of (I 0 -I)/I 0 on the concentration of Hg2+ ions within the range of 0–75 μM (G, H) Linear plot of Hg2+ concentration against the (I 0 -I)/I 0 value of colloidal motors for emission peak at 460 nm. (I) Quantitative detection of Hg2+ in water (a) and Hg2+ in milk (b) (The left columns of group a and group b were obtained by colloidal motors detection, and the right columns of group a and group b were obtained by atomic fluorescence spectrometry).

β-gal-powered colloidal motors were used for visual detection of Hg2+ by smartphone. (A) Schematic diagram of β-gal-powered colloidal motors used for visual detection of Hg2+. (B) Fluorescence pictures of the colloidal motors under UV light in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+. (C) Calibration curve of the colloidal motors between fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Hg2+ (0–50 μM). (D) Photo of the test strip of β-gal-powered colloidal motors for different concentrations of Hg2+.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive and rapid detection of Hg2+ in milk based on β-galactosidase driven colloidal motors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medicine and Health, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Medicine and Health, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin 150001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering College, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot 010021, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering College, Inner Mongolia University, Hohhot 010021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we present a new β-galactosidase-powered Janus colloidal motor for the active and rapid detection of Hg2+ in milk. The colloidal motor was fabricated by depositing a thin gold layer on one side of the self-assembled polyelectrolyte capsule, followed by immobilizing β-galactosidase and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots asymmetrically on the other side of the polyelectrolyte capsule. β-Galactosidase on the surface of the gold side can decompose the lactose in milk into glucose and galactose, leading to active motion. Furthermore, since nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots on the colloidal motor’s surface can be quenched by Hg2+ in milk, such colloidal motors are found to serve as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for the sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions. Benefiting from the efficient movement of the colloidal motors, the interaction between the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and the quenched substrate is greatly enhanced, thus improving the detection efficiency and accuracy of Hg2+. Under the optimized conditions, the colloidal motors achieved a linear detection range of 0.031–15 μM and the low detection limit of 9.1 nM. The colloidal motors are proving to be an attractive multifunctional sensor platform, providing a valuable means of monitoring Hg2+ contamination in dairy products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is considered one of the most hazardous heavy metals in the environment, which is easy to migrate, not easy to degrade, and has a strong bioaccumulation, posing a significant threat not only to human health but also to ecosystems and wildlife (Huang et al., 2024; Kaewnok et al., 2021; Z. Liu et al., 2020; Yang, Li, Liu, Ubaid Ali, Ding, & Wang, 2023). The ingestion of mercury-contaminated feed and drinking water by cow results in bioaccumulation of mercury within their bodies, which can subsequently be transferred to milk (Wang, Qu, Wang, Dong, & Zheng, 2022; Zhou, Zheng, Su, Wang, & Soyeurt, 2019). Consumption of this dairy product, mercury ions in the human body will accumulate to a certain extent and will show toxic effects, thus endangering human health (Arisekar et al., 2024; Tan, Wang, Yu, Pan, & Liu, 2024; Wang, Zhang, Han, Zhang, Wang, & Chen, 2021; Yuan, Yang, Lv, & Zhu, 2022). Therefore, the detection of Hg2+ content in milk is of great significance. However, the traditional quantitative methods, such as atomic absorption spectrometry, cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry, gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography, and electrochemical methods, are complex, high cost, and strong technical expertise, which make it very difficult to detect Hg2+ in real-time (Atasoy, Yildiz, Kula, & Vaizoğullar, 2023; Malik, Bashir, Qureashi, & Pandith, 2019; Nolvachai, Kulsing, & Marriott, 2017). Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a rapid, convenient, low-cost, and ultra-sensitive tool for assessing trace Hg2+ contamination in dairy products.

Current progress in the field of artificial colloidal motors holds considerable promise for novel “real-time” analytical measurements of Hg2+ in different samples, avoiding existing drawbacks of current methodologies (Hu, Li, & Sun, 2022; Ma, Bai, & Zheng, 2022; Parmar, Vilela, Villa, Wang, & Sánchez, 2018; Wang et al., 2020; Wu, Balasubramanian, Kagan, Manesh, Campuzano, & Wang, 2010). The self-propulsion ability of colloidal motors induces efficient fluid mixing, which is a very useful property for environments that do not allow mechanical agitation or at the microscopic level (Luo, Chen, Liang, Xu, & Zhang, 2023; Peng, Men, Tu, Chen, & Wilson, 2018; Venugopalan, Esteban-Fernández de Ávila, Pal, Ghosh, & Wang, 2020; Yong Wang, Duan, Zhou, Li, Wang, & Ma, 2019). This colloidal motor-induced mixing has been shown to overcome diffusion-limited reactions, improve the interaction between its active surface and the target, increase the reaction yield and shorten the processing time (Arque, Romero-Rivera, Feixas, Patino, Osuna, & Sanchez, 2019; Dou, Liu, Chen, Wang, Zheng, & Xu, 2025; Keller, Teora, Hu, Nijemeisland, & Wilson, 2018). The D. Rojas group proposed a novel Janus micromotor-based method for the direct determination of diphenyl phthalate (DPP) in food and biological samples (Rojas, Jurado-Sánchez, & Escarpa, 2016). The Mg/Au Janus micromotor generates hydrogen microbubbles and hydroxyl ions along the self-motion of the sample for DPP degradation. The increased fluid transport significantly improves the analytical signal and increases the sensitivity while reducing the detection potential. The Wang group synthesized near-infrared (NIR)-powered Janus colloidal motors composed of Au nanorods and periodic mesoporous organo-silica microspheres (AuNR/PMO JNMs) can be used for lateral flow test strip (LFTS) to accurately detect miRNA, and the detection limit is 12.22 times that of static probe LFTS (Chen, Ma, Xing, Wang, & Zheng, 2023). So, Current advances in the field of artificial micro/colloidal motors offer considerable promise for novel “real-time” analytical measurements of Hg2+ in different samples, avoiding the existing pitfalls of conventional methods.

Herein, we demonstrate a β-galactosidase-powered Janus polyelectrolyte microcapsules colloidal motors (β-gal-powered colloidal motors), for accurate detection of Hg2+ in milk. Firstly, the microcapsules were synthesized by layer-by-layer self-assembly method, and then the swimming β-gal-powered colloidal motor probe was constructed by asymmetric modification of β-galactosidase and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) that can be specifically quenched by Hg2+. Colloidal motors modified with β-galactosidase on one side were able to use lactose in milk as fuel to achieve efficient self-propulsion in the test sample. Due to the efficient motion of the colloidal motors, the interaction between the N-CQDs and Hg2+ is greatly enhanced. By detecting the fluorescence intensity of samples added with different Hg2+ concentrations, the accurate detection of Hg2+ concentration in milk can be achieved. Moreover, the fluorescent standard array and smartphone can be used for rapid and intuitive detection of Hg2+ based on β-gal-powered colloidal motors. Combination of Hg2+ detection of N-CQDs and self-actuation generated by lactose catalysis in milk, colloidal motor proves an attractive multi-functional sensor platform for future food detection.

The silica spheres with a diameter of 2.5 μm were obtained from Baseline Chromtech Research Center, Tianjin, China, poly (allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH, Mw = 50,000), poly (styrenesulfonate) sodium (PSS, Mw = 70,000), sulfo-NHS, 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC), β-Galactosidase from Escherichia coli, O-Nitrophenol, 2-Nitrophenyl-β-d-galactopyranoside, mercury single element standard solution (1 % HNO3), fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), lactose, NaCl, hydrofluoric acid (HF), glucose detection kit, KCl, CuCl2, NiCl2, MnCl2, CoCl2, CrCl3, CaCl2, AgCl, Pb(NO3)2, PBS (PH = 7.4, 0.1 M), folic acid, ethylene glycol.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were taken on a HT7820 (HITACHI). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using the ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer to determine the elemental composition and chemical groups. The fluorescence spectrum was performed by FL 6500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PERKINELMER), with an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, a voltage of 400 V, a slit of 5 nm, and a scanning speed of 300 nm/min. Ultraviolet–visible light (UV–vis) absorption spectra were characterized by the U-3900 ultraviolet–visible spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was determined from 400 to 4000 cm−1 using the KBr method using a Nicolet iS50 FTIR spectrometer (FTIR, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA, USA). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to characterize the morphology and size of the JCPS motor (SUPRA55). The high-speed centrifuge (TG16-WS, China) was purchased from Hunan Xiangyi Laboratory Instrument Development Co., Ltd.

N-CQDs were synthesized by simplifying previous studies (Zhang & Chen, 2014), Ethylene glycol (5 mL) and folic acid (0.1 g) were mixed with ultrapure water (10 mL) by ultrasound. The solution was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After the reaction, the reactor was naturally cooled to room temperature. The brown-yellow product was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 8–14 kDa), and the yellow solution obtained after dialysis was the target product.

Artificial hollow microcapsules were prepared by layer-by-layer self-assembly using 2.5 μm silica particles as the template and polyelectrolyte PSS/PAH as wall material, add 2 mg/L PSS aqueous solution containing 2 mg/L NaCl as described above, oscillate on an oscillator for 10 min, centrifuge to remove the supernatant, and then wash the precipitate with 2 mg/L NaCl liquid for three times, Then the template was added into 2 mg/L PAH aqueous solution containing 2 mg/L NaCl and oscillated on an oscillator for 10 min, then the supernatant was removed by centrifugation, and the precipitate was washed with 2 mg/L NaCl solution for three times to complete the assembly of a polyelectrolyte bilayer, Repeat the above steps to adsorb PAH and PSS alternately in turn until five layers of microcapsule skeleton assembled on silica particle template are assembled. The prepared silica particles with five double-layer polyelectrolytes were laid flat on a hydrophilic glass to form a single-layer film, which was placed on a sputtering target at an angle of 45 degrees and sputtered vertically and uniformly on a sample sputtering and spraying instrument for vacuum deposition of gold under the condition of 20 mA, 90 s, After the single-layer glass is dispersed, the polyelectrolyte spheres with gold on the outermost layer at one end are obtained, Set aside after washing with deionized water. The silica particles assembled with polyelectrolyte treated in the above steps were added into hydrofluoric acid solution with a concentration of 3 M and shaken slightly for 15 min. After centrifugal separation, the supernatant was discarded, and the precipitate obtained was washed with deionized water three times. The polymer microcapsules with regular structure were stored in water for later use if the enucleation is complete, the enucleation can be repeated 2–3 times.

The microcapsules were placed in a PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) containing 100 mM sulfo-NHS and 400 mM EDC for 12 h, They were then washed three times with 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4), and then incubated in 2 mg/ml β-galactosidase solution at 37 °C for 12 h, The precipitate was washed three times with 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4). The microcapsule colloidal motors modified with β-galactosidase and gold metal modified at one end after removing the core with hydrofluoric acid were stored in PBS buffer at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption of N-CQDs by electrostatic interaction
ce:section.ce:para: An additional layer of PSS was assembled on the β-galactosidase modified microcapsule colloidal motor. Since PSS has a negative charge and the synthesized N-CQDs have a positive charge, the final sample can be obtained by modifying N-CQDs on the microcapsule colloidal motors by electrostatic adsorption. The β-gal-powered colloidal motors were purified by the three centrifugation/water washing steps. All obtained capsule solutions were stored at 4 °C.

The trajectories of micromotors were recorded by an Olympus BX53 fluorescence microscope (20 × or 40 × objectives). After recording the position information state of the microcapsule colloidal motors under different conditions, using Image J and other software, the motion trajectory of Janus colloidal motor in motion video is statistically calculated, such as its Mean-Squared Displacement (MSD), diffusion coefficient, average velocity, and other data.

Samples of three brands of commercial milk were purchased from Inner Mongolia Mengniu Dairy (Group) Limited by Share Ltd, Inner Mongolia Yili Industrial Group Limited by Share Ltd and Nestle (China) Ltd. Simultaneously, Take three independent replicates of the same brand of milk. β-gal-powered colloidal motors and milk samples mixed with different concentrations of Hg2+ were added into a 0.35 M microdish and photos were taken with mobile phone under 360 nm UV irradiation. PhotoMetrix software was used to calculate the average fluorescence intensity of each picture and draw a standard curve, and then semi-quantitative detection of Hg2+ in milk could be carried out.

Milk sample processing methods: 2 mL of milk was mixed with 600 µL of zinc acetate-phosphotungstic acid solution. After stirring for 10 min, the mixture was filtered and 1 mL of the filtrate was mixed with 5 mL of 0.1 M NaOH. Next, the solution was diluted to 20 mL with deionized water and allowed to precipitate. Finally, 10 mL of the supernatant was diluted to 20 mL and used as a sample (Maria-Hormigos, Jurado-Sánchez, & Escarpa, 2018).
The relationship between Hg2+ concentration and its emission spectrum were investigated by measuring the emission spectrum of β-gal-powered colloidal motors at 360 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Efficiency and selectivity of Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: The efficiency of β-gal-powered colloidal motors was detected by comparing the quenching efficiency of β-gal-powered colloidal motors and ordinary static particles in 0.05 g/mL lactose at different time periods. Other ions such as Al3+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Ag+ were added to determine the selectivity of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors to Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of β-gal-powered colloidal motors
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare β-gal-powered colloidal motors, five bilayers of negatively charged poly (styrene sulfonate) sodium (PSS) and positively charged poly(allylamine hydrochloride) (PAH) were alternately assembled on the surface of 2.5 μm silica particles via a layer-by-layer self-assembly technique (Liu, Wu, Li, Yang, Wu, & He, 2023). Second, (PSS/PAH)5-coated silica particles were dropped onto a flat hydrophilic slide to form a monolayer, and then 5 nm thick gold was sputtering on the particle monolayer. After removal of the silica template, hollow Janus (PSS/PAH)5 capsules were obtained. β-Galactosidase was immobilized to the gold surface of the particles by EDC/NHS cross-linking. Finally, water-soluble N-CQDs were prepared by a simple and economical hydrothermal method using folic acid as a carbon and nitrogen source (Fig. S1), electrostatic interactions were used to immobilize N-CQDs on the particle-exposed surface to obtain β-gal-powered colloidal motors.

As seen in Fig. 1 B, the morphologies of N-CQDs dispersed in the water are highly monodispersed spherical dots with a mean diameter of 3.52 ± 1.21 nm. By X-ray diffraction (XRD) method, the crystal structure of N-CQDs was obtained, it can be seen in Fig. S2A that the XRD pattern of N-CQDs shows a wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 22.10, indicating that the carbon in N-CQDs had amorphous properties. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer was used to study the surface structure of N-CQDs, and multiple functional groups were found on the surface of N-CQDs. The peak at 1435 cm−1 is caused by the stretching vibration of C–N, and peaks at 1566 and 3080 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending and stretching vibration of N–H, characteristic peaks at 1659 and 2933 cm−1 appeared, which are of the stretching vibration of C=O and C–H, respectively, while the peak at 3283 cm−1 was caused by O–H stretching vibration (Fig. S2B). The elemental composition and chemical states of the prepared N-CQDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full XPS spectrum of N-CQDs indicated three peaks at 285.08 eV, 399.08 eV, and 531.08 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively, in which C, N, O atoms accounted for 69.69 %, 13.46 % and 16.85 %, indicating the high doping concentration of nitrogen in the N-CQDs with folic acid as precursor. Furthermore, the change in surface charge confirmed each modification step (Fig. 1C). The potential of β-galactosidase immobilized on the motor raised to −27.9 mV, demonstrated successful assembly of the β-galactosidase. After N-CQDs were modified on the surface of the colloidal motor, the surface charge increased to 31.5 mV, this is due to the positive surface charge of N-CQDs, which proves the successful modification of N-CQDs. The scanning electron microscopic (SEM) image (Fig. 1D) and transmission electron microscopic (TEM) image (Fig. 1E) show a 2.5 μm β-gal-powered colloidal motor after sputtering with Au. One can see that the Au shell with irregular edges partially covers the polymer-coated particles, providing them a Janus feature. Furthermore, the SEM image and corresponding energy dispersive spectroscopic (EDS) mapping of Au and S elements (Fig. 1D) confirm the existence and asymmetric distribution of metal and β-galactosidase. The particle size distribution measured with a dynamic light scattering instrument (Fig. 1F) further shows that the resulting β-gal-powered colloidal motors exhibit monodispersity and a uniform size with an average diameter of 2.4981 ± 0.02591 μm. The surface structure of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors after β-galactosidase modification was confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy. The C=O stretching vibration peak of β-galactosidase was 1540 cm−1, indicating the existence of β-galactosidase on the surface of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors. At the same time, two new peaks in 2971 cm−1 and 2923 cm−1 were observed, which proved that β-galactosidase was covalently linked to the colloidal motors (Fig. 1G). The confocal laser scanning microscope image shows the Janus distribution of β-galactosidase on colloidal motors and further verified the successful Janus modification of β-galactosidase (Fig. 1H). It can be clearly seen under the fluorescence microscope that the fluorescence of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors is effectively quenched after the addition of Hg2+, reflecting Hg2+ can quench the fluorescence of the colloidal motors (Fig. 1I). The reason for the Hg2+ quenched β-gal-powered colloidal motors was further investigated by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy. Upon addition of 30 μM Hg2+, the UV–vis absorption of β-gal-powered colloidal motors changed significantly, that is the absorption at 360 nm became weaker, which demonstrates the presence of Hg2+ can influence the surface states of the N-CQDs (Fig. 1J). Therefore, β-gal-powered colloidal motors can be effectively quenched by Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Movement study of β-gal-powered colloidal motors
ce:section.ce:para: The Janus distribution of β-galactosidase over the surface of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors results in asymmetric biocatalytic decomposition of lactose into glucose and galactose. A concentration gradient and active directional flow of the reaction products around the β-gal-powered colloidal motors are created, which drives the β-gal-powered colloidal motors to carry on self-diffusiophoretic propulsion (Fig. 2 A).

We selected six different lactose concentration (0, 0.02, 0.04, 0.06, 0.08 g/mL and simulated milk) to verify the colloidal motors can movement in the milk, The typical trajectories of β-gal-powered colloidal motors are shown in Fig. 2B, which illustrates that the motions of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors are clearly enhanced with increasing lactose concentration. Then, we investigated this enhanced motion by calculating the mean displacement (MSD) (taken from the corresponding Video S1). The plots of MSD in Fig. 2C display a linear increase in the absence of lactose, and a parabolic increase in the presence of lactose (0.02–0.08 g/mL), reflecting that self-diffusion enhances the motility of the colloidal motors. To assess the effective movement time of the colloidal motors, a certain amount of colloidal motors was added to lactose solution, and the concentration of glucose decomposed from lactose was recorded at different times. It can be seen that the glucose concentration continues to rise for 20 min, reflecting that the colloidal motors can run for 20 min (Fig. 2D). The long-time translational diffusion coefficient (DL) was evaluated by fitting the MSD curves to the equation (DL = MSD/4Dt) in Fig. 2E. Compared with Brownian motion in the absence of fuel, the DL of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors was significantly enhanced to 0.143 ± 0.0192 μm2/s (simulated milk), further confirming that the β-gal-powered colloidal motors are effectively driven by the asymmetric enzyme-catalytic chemical reactions. A similar trend was observed in Fig. 2F, wherein the velocity of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors (2.04 ± 0.11) μm/s exceeded the Brownian motion speed (0.074 ± 0.047) μm/s at a concentration of 0.08 g/mL. This propulsion behavior demonstrates the move ability of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors toward expanded possibilities in diverse environments. Overall, these results indicate that the β-gal-powered colloidal motors exhibit effective self-propulsion in biological media in the presence of lactose, reflecting that the self-propulsion of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors is driven by the concentration gradient and active directional flow, generated via the asymmetric biocatalytic decomposition of lactose.

To enhance the motility of the colloidal motors, it is essential to optimize key enzyme operating parameters such as temperature and pH stability. Subsequently, the activity of free enzyme and β-gal-powered colloidal motors was evaluated under different pH (5.5–9.5) values. As shown in Fig. 2G, pH exerts a strong influence in enzyme activity, with optimal enzyme activity observed at a narrow pH range (7.5–8.5) for immobilized enzyme. Colloidal motors movements were recorded in buffer solutions with different pH values, The colloidal motors achieved the highest MSD at pH 8.5 and the corresponding DL value reached the maximum. Meanwhile, we investigated the optimum temperature of the catalytic reaction between the free enzyme and β-gal-powered colloidal motors to find the optimum conditions. It was found that the activity of free enzyme with β-gal-powered colloidal motors increased from low to high between 25 °C and 50 °C. When the temperature continued to increase, the activities of the two enzymes decreased significantly, indicating that the optimal reaction temperature was about 35 °C, and at this temperature, the stability of the enzyme was good. In addition, the optimal reaction temperature of free enzyme and β-gal-powered colloidal motors was the same, indicating that the β-Gal assembly of the colloidal motors did not affect the properties of the enzyme (Fig. S4). Overall, the pH and temperature stability of β-gal-powered colloidal motors is good, which proved its applicability as detection probe under larger experiment conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: β-gal-powered colloidal motors-based fluorescent chemosensor for sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the unique fluorescence properties of N-CQDs (Fig. S5), the β-gal-powered colloidal motors have two excitation peaks around 225 and 360 nm. When excited at 360 nm, the β-gal-powered colloidal motors exhibited strong fluorescence emission centered at 460 nm (Fig. 3 A). The specificity of a fluorescent probe to detect the target analyte is its essential property. The specificity of colloidal motors was evaluated by inspecting its fluorescence signaling at the wavelength of 460 nm after adding various metal cations (Ca2+, Cr2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Na+, K+, Ni2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Pb2+) into the probe solution. No significant change in fluorescence intensity was observed after the addition of various interferents (30 μM). However, the fluorescence intensity of colloidal motors was significantly reduced after 30 μM Hg2+ addition, which proved that other interferents did not cause fluorescence quenching, demonstrating that colloidal motors can selectively recognize Hg2+ in the complex aqueous systems (Fig. 3B). Moreover, the anti-interference experiment was performed by immersing the colloidal motors into the mixture solution containing not only targeted analytes Hg2+, but also interferential metal ions (Fig. 3C), indicating that the β-gal-powered colloidal motors are insensitive to other metal ions but selective to Hg2+ in the mixtures. Therefore, the as-prepared β-gal-powered colloidal motors possessed a remarkable selectivity towards Hg2+, making them highly suitable for application in Hg2+ detection sensors.

Furthermore, the time required for the colloidal motors to be quenched by Hg2+ in the sample solution was also studied. Interestingly, only 55 % of the fluorescence quenching was achieved when using static colloidal motors (as controls) and took at least 12 min, while 61 % of the fluorescence quenching was achieved in only 4 min using dynamic colloidal motors. However, the quenching effect of using N-CQDs alone lies between the two (Fig. 3D). These results confirm that both movement of the colloidal motors around the small volume of sample and the mixing effect due to the localized fluid convection and vortex flow associated with the fast movement of the colloidal motors. It is further demonstrated that the self-diffusion phoretic propulsion of β-gal-powered colloidal motors overcomes the limitation of passive diffusion and enhances the interaction between the reactors.

To demonstrate the performance of the developed colloidal motors sensor for Hg2+ detection, the detection range and LOD were investigated. The fluorescence spectra of the obtained β-gal-powered colloidal motors were measured after incubating them with different concentrations of Hg2+ for 4 min. As depicted in Fig. 3E, it can be seen that the fluorescence intensity (excited at 360 nm) gradually decreased with increasing the concentration of Hg2+ from 5 to 50 μM. The calibration curve for determining Hg2+ in the milk was obtained by plotting the values of (I 0 –I)/I 0 versus the concentrations of Hg2+. To clarify, Fig. 3G shows the detection of Hg2+ in the micromolar range, while Fig. 3H presents the detection in the nanomolar range. We separated the two figures to obtain two successive linear ranges, initially in the range of 0.031 μM to 15 μM (Fig. 3G) and secondly for 31 to 200 nM (Fig. 3H). The corresponding limit of detection (LOD) is calculated to be 9.1 nM according to the equation LOD = (3SD)/k, which was far lower than the national standard of milk and dairy products total mercury content of 0.01 mg/kg (∼ 10 μM). Thus, the minuscule detection of Hg2+ ions, even in the nanomolar range, is possible with the synthesized β-gal-powered colloidal motors. Moreover, β-gal-powered colloidal motors have better sensitivity than previously reported fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection (Table S1). In order to verify whether β-gal-powered colloidal motors can be further applied to actual samples, we used the standard addition method to add Hg2+ to the real samples with final concentrations of Hg2+ of 8.5 μM. Meanwhile, we also employed atomic fluorescence spectroscopy (AFS) analysis as a standard control to evaluate the accuracy and reliability of the colloidal motors. As shown in Fig. 3I, the detection results obtained using colloidal motors were in excellent agreement with those of AFS. This demonstrates the superior performance of colloidal motors, which not only match the sensitivity and precision of traditional techniques like AFS but also offer the added advantage of autonomous movement, enabling more efficient and localized detection without the need for external intervention. Moreover, the quantitative detection results were nearly identical to the standard value in both water and milk samples, indicating the feasibility of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors for the detection of Hg2+ in actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building a smartphone sensing platform for rapid detection of Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve on-site detection without delay, it is necessary to develop an easy-to-use and cost-effective portable device for detection recording and processing experimental data. N-CQDs showed excellent color evolution effects and could be combined with smartphone to create a portable visual sensing platform for quantifying Hg2+. So, we built an Hg2+ testing platform composed of smartphone, closed box, light source, mobile power supply, and sample bottle, to lay a good foundation for the in-site detection of Hg2+. β-gal-powered colloidal motors move in a self-diffusion permanent way in milk to accelerate the combination with Hg2+ in solution, which can quench the N-CQDs adsorbed on the colloidal motors (Fig. 4 A). Under the UV light of 360 nm, the weakening of fluorescence intensity can be clearly seen with the naked eye (Fig. 4B). Therefore, colloidal motors exhibited an excellent fluorescence evolution effect and could be combined with a smartphone to create a portable visual sensing platform for quantifying Hg2+.

A fluorescence standard array including fluorescence standard images of different Hg2+ concentrations (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μM) was obtained by smartphone. The fluorescence brightness of β-gal-powered colloidal motors decreased regularly with the addition of more and more Hg2+, which was observed by the naked eye. The fluorescence standard array can be used for visual detection of Hg2+ based on the fluorescence brightness. The 96-well plate was utilized to establish a fluorescent standard array, streamlining the procedure and reducing reagent consumption. Fluorescent standard arrays offer a rapid, straightforward, and intuitive method for Hg2+ detection. By further exploring the test strip detection experiment on the basis of the previous experiment. β-gal-powered colloidal motors and different concentrations (0–50 μM) of Hg2+ were added to the test strip. The fluorescence intensity of the test strip was observed under ultraviolet lamp irradiation (Fig. 4D). It can be seen that with the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased, indicating β-gal-powered colloidal motors can quickly and conveniently detect Hg2+ by dipstick. The results showed that portable and visual Hg2+ quantitative detection was satisfactory for this smartphone sensing platform. PhotoMetrix software was further used to automatically capture fluorescence intensity data from photographs and convert them to numerical values. The Hg2+ content in this sample was determined by the linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity value and the Hg2+ concentration. In the range of 0–50 μM, the fitted linear equation shown in Fig. 4C was y = −1.8204 X + 131.17 (R2 = 0.9914). The results show that the smartphone sensing platform can achieve Hg2+ semi-quantitative detection and visual detection.

In summary, we developed an active sensing system for the rapid and sensitive detection of Hg2+ in milk based on endogenous fuel-driven colloidal motors. Due to the autonomous motion capability of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors, motion-induced active mixing does not require external forced convection to enhance mass transfer, which improves detection limit, reduces detection time, and avoids tedious sample preparation procedures. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I 0 –I)/I 0 changed gradually with the addition of Hg2+. The colloidal motors showed good analytical performance, with a linear range of 0.031–15 μM and a limit of detection as low as 9.1 nM. Due to the self-drive ability of the β-gal-powered colloidal motors, the detection time was reduced to 4 min. Based on fluorescence intensity change, a low-cost portable sensing platform was designed for fast and highly sensitive on-site visual monitoring of Hg2+ in milk. This method exhibits high sensitivity and selectivity, which pave a new way for the detection of Hg2+ in dairy products and help to guarantee milk safety.

Sheng Kang: Writing – original draft, Project administration. Yong Zheng: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Baohua Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Yingjie Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Qiang He: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Nature Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22172044, U23A20342), Key R&D Program of Heilongjiang Province, China (Grant No. 2022ZX02C23), Heilongjiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. YQ2022B005).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodres.2025.115977.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
